Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - TWO INSTANCES OF INTERPRETABLE NEURAL NET WORK FOR UNIVERSAL APPROXIMATIONS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

This paper proposes two bottom-up interpretable neural network (NN) constructions for universal approximation, namely Triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN). The notable properties are (1) resistance to catastrophic forgetting (2) existence of proof for arbitrarily high accuracies on training dataset (3) for an input _x_ , users can identify specific samples of training data whose activation “fingerprints” are similar to that of _x_ ’s activations. Users can also identify samples that are out of distribution.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Artificial neural networks (NN) have recently seen successful applications in many fields. Modern deep neural network (DNN) architecture, usually trained through the backpropagation mechanism, has been called a black-box because of its lack of interpretability. To tackle this issue, various studies have been performed to understand how a NN works; see the following surveys Arrieta et al. (2020); Gilpin et al. (2018); Tjoa & Guan (2020); Wiegreffe & Marasovi´c (2021). This paper primarily proposes two interpretable models, namely triangularly-constructed NN (TNN) and Semi-Quantized Activation NN (SQANN). Both possess the following three notable properties: (1) Resistance to _catastrophic forgetting_ . (2) Mathematical proofs for arbitrarily high accuracy on training datasets; experimentally demonstrable with python code and simple common datasets (see supp. materials). (3) Detection of out-of-distribution samples through weak activations.
**Concept disambiguation** . Several concepts have multiple possible definitions. We clarify the definitions used in this paper.
1. Interpretability. We consider only fine-grained interpretation, i.e. we look at the meaning of each single neuron or its activation in our models.

2. Universal approximation. Readers might be familiar with universal approximation of functions on certain conditions, e.g. compact sets. Our models can be more general, e.g. user can freely choose the interpolation function between 2 activations based on knowledge of the local manifold. The function can even be pathological. See appendix A.2.1.

3. Catastrophic forgetting: the tendency for knowledge of previously learned dataset to be abruptly lost as information relevant to a new dataset is incorporated. This definition is a slightly nuanced version of Kirkpatrick et al. (2017). Hence, our models’ resistance to catastrophic forgetting is the following. Given a training dataset _D_ accurately modeled by an architecture _M_ , a new dataset _D[′]_ (especially new, out of distribution dataset) can be incorporated into _M_ without losing accuracy on _D_ . See appendix A.2.2 (analogy to biological system included).

**Related works and interpretability issues** . Recent remarkable studies on universal approximators include the Deep Narrow Network by Kidger & Lyons (2020), DeepONet universal approximation for operators by Lu et al. (2021) and the Broad Learning System by Chen et al. (2019); Hanin (2019); Park et al. (2021); Johnson (2019). While insightful, they do not directly address the eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) issue, especially the blackbox property of the DNN. Similarly, a number of classical papers provide theoretical insights for NN as universal approximators, but interpretability, transparency and fairness issues are not their main focus. The universal approximation theorem by Cybenko (1989) asserts that a NN with a single hidden layer can approximate any function to arbitrarily small error under common conditions, proven by asserting the density of that set of NN

in the function space using classic mathematical theorems. In particular, its theorem 1 uses an abstract proof by contradiction. From the proof, it is not easy to observe the internal mechanism of a NN in a straight-forward manner; consequently modern works that depend on it (e.g. Deep Narrow Network) might inherit the blackbox property. Bottom-up constructions for function approximation using NN then emerged, though they also lack the interpretability (see appendix A.3 for more related works). Also consider a demonstration in Nielsen (2015) that could help improve our understanding of universal approximation.

**Outline** . This paper is arranged as the following. Section 2 shows explicit TNN construction, related results, including _a pencil-and-paper example_ for pedagogical purpose. Likewise, section 3 shows SQANN construction, statements regarding SQANN, _another pencil-and-paper example_ , then experimental results of its application, before we conclude the paper with limitations and future works. Python codes and clearer figures are fully available in supp. materials (also see appendix).

## 2 TRIANGULARLY-CONSTRUCTED NN (TNN)

TNN is the prototype NN for our interpretable universal approximator. SQANN (next section) partially borrows the concept from TNN which will be useful as an easy and manageable illustration to deliver the following ideas: (1) organized activations of neurons and (2) the retrieval of _α_ values as the outputs. The model is _TNN_ ( _x_ ) = _α[T] σ_ ( _Wx_ + _b_ ) where _x ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], _α, b ∈_ R _[N]_ and _W ∈_ R _[N]_ , where we use sigmoid function _σ_ for simplicity. We start with a simple scalar function _y_ = _f_ ( _x_ ) _∈_ R for _x ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], thus TNN’s interpretability can be illustrated very clearly.

**Assumption: Linear Ordering** . It is constructed on a linearly ordered dataset containing _N_ samples _{_ ( _x_[(] _[k]_[)] _, y_[(] _[k]_[)] ) _∈_ R _[n] ×_ R _}[N] k_ =1[such][that] _[x]_[(] _[N]_[)] _[<][x]_[(] _[N][−]_[1)] _[<][· · ·][<][x]_[(1)][and] _[y]_[(] _[k]_[)][=] _[f]_[(] _[x]_[(] _[k]_[)][)][,] _[f]_ the true function that TNN will approximate. The interpretability comes from the linear ordering property where higher value of _x_ ( _≈_ 1) will activate more neurons while lower values will activate less neurons as shown in fig 1(A). Then _α_ values will be retrieved in a continuous way through dot product, eventually used to compute the output for prediction. In time series, such as ECG (Electrocardiogram), signals can be approximated point-wise (although it is still preferable to have a noise model during preprocessing to prevent overfitting the noise). Meaningful interpretation can be given, for example, by mapping PQRST segments from ECG to specific neurons within TNN, giving some neurons specific meaning and thus interpretability. For more remarks and definition of formal _linear ordering_ etc, see appendix A.4.

We proceed with SQANN, a universal approximation inspired by TNN that allows multi-dimensional input, multi-layer stacking of neurons based on relative strength of neuron activations.

## 3 SEMI-QUANTIZED ACTIVATION NEURAL NETWORK (SQANN)

SQANN architecture is a multi-dimensional universal approximator which (1) retains TNN’s idea of using an organized sequence of activations to retrieve _α_ , (2) remotely resembles a Radial Basis Function, but (3) has deep neural network properties, such as the possibility of deep learning (multiple constructed layers) and neuron activations. The difference is, a neuron in SQANN corresponds exactly to a data sample as SQANN stores its “fingerprints” as neurons’ nuclei as shown in fig. 3(B). The neurons can be activated with different kinds of responses: (1) distinct peaks and (2) half-activations and (3) weak/zero activations, made possible by double selective activation _σdsa_ . They are illustrated in fig. 3(A) and the sketch of proof of proposition 3 in appendix A.5.4, _intuition behind σdsa_ .

The aforementioned distinct types of activation are key to SQANN’s main result and give the model an interpretability at least in the following sense: samples are highly/moderately/not recognizable if their activation patterns strongly/moderately/weakly resemble the activation patterns of an existing training sample _x_ , where identifications are facilitated by the distinct regions of _σdsa_ . The design is “semi” quantized since _σdsa_ has approximately “distinct” levels yet remains continuous. Since SQANN incorporates multi-layer structure, it avoids being a non-generalizing model that nearest neighbours methods suffer from; see the remark in (Pedregosa et al. (2011a)).

## 3.1 SQANN CONSTRUCTION

**Outline of SQANN construction with interpretations** . SQANN is constructed without optimization like gradient descent. Each indexed training data sample is converted into a “fingerprint” or pattern of neuron activations, which undergoes one of the following:

1. _Admission to layer k_ . Sample’s new/distinct fingerprint is added into layer _k_ if the sample weakly activates existing nodes in the layer ( _∀j,_ [ _vk_ ] _j < τad_ ) and no collision occurs; see fig. 3(D.1).

2. _Collision_ . A sample activates one or more neurons strongly i.e. _∃j, k,_ [ _vk_ ] _j > τact_ . The earliest layer where collision occurs is denoted _lc_ . Such sample is integrated into _lc_ , thus very similar samples are _selectively clustered_ . See fig. 3(D.2); the concept is also illustrated in the sketch of proof for proposition 3, appendix A.5.4.

3. _Filtering into deeper layer_ occurs when neither of the above occurs (no strong activation, some moderate activations). Such sample has features loosely similar to previously seen samples, but we need to filter them further to distinguish its finer features.

When strong activation is detected, the signal will be redirected to the dark red nucleus _αl[<k>]_ . (C) SQANN used for a simple classification. At this point, it may be clearer to readers how SQANN is constructed. In short, for each layer, _representative activations_ become the neurons of the layer. In layer 1, representatives activations are the samples themselves. In deeper layers, they are activations propagated to the layer.

**Layer k construction** . Layer _k_ construction is similar to layer 1 construction, except collision could occur at any layer _lc ≤ k_ (next paragraph). Initialize by first checking _vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ , the activation of _u[<]_[1] _[>]_ at layer _k_ for collision; if collision occurs, see next paragraph, otherwise, set _Nk_ = ( _ηk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) _, αk_ = ( _yk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) i.e. _ηk[<]_[1] _[>] ← vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ . Similar to layer 1 construction, perform _check Nk activation_ on ( _u[<i>] , y[<i>]_ ) for _i >_ 1 by computing activation _vk[<i>]_ and checking it against the existing nodes. One of the three cases occur (1) admission, when [ _vk[<i>]_ ] _j < τad_ for all _j_ = 1 _, . . . , nk_ and no collision (2) collision, when there exists index _j_ at a _collided layer lc ≤ k_ such that [ _vl[<i>] c_ ] _j > τact_ or (3) otherwise. If (1) occurs, the activation _vk[<i>]_ is added as a new neuron to the layer, _Nk → concat_ ( _Nk, vk[<i>]_ ). If (3) occurs, filter the data sample for deeper layer. Assuming no collision, the process is repeated for the next unused data sample _u[<i>]_ until all remaining data samples are checked. Once done, repeat the process for layer _k_ + 1 construction.

Suppose collision happens when we check _u[<m>]_ at layer _lc_ , we use the collision resolution mechanism. We destroy all layers _l > lc_ and put the collided sample into _lc_ , i.e. _Nlc → concat_ ( _Nlc , vk[<m>]_ ) and _αlc → concat_ ( _αlc , yk[<m>]_ ) (push-node in the pseudo code). No layer will be destroyed if _lc_ = _k_ , the current layer. The data samples used in each destroyed layer are returned to the list of unused samples in the same order they have been used during the construction (returnindex() in the pseudo code); we refer to this as _order integrity_ . Once the colliding sample is added to _lc_ , effectively, the strong activation in this layer is now overshadowed by maximum activation ( _selective clustering_ in action), since the exact neuron is now included as the representative of itself and its locality. This is possibly a practically inefficient process, since we tear down intermediate layers, but we only prioritize the completion of the construction for now.

Otherwise, for subsequent layer _k_ , recursively compute [ _vk_ ] _j_ = _σdsa_ � _||vk−_ 1 _− ηk[<j>] ||_ � for all _j_ = 1 _, . . . , nk_ . Each training sample _x_ admitted to layer _Nk_ leaves a fingerprint, in the sense that it has a collection of activations _{vl|l_ = 1 _, . . . , k}_ as it is SQANN-propagated through the neural network. **Completing construction** . Due to collisions, readers might wonder if the construction will complete at all. During collision, layers are torn down and reconstructed. Suppose during layer _k_ construction, collision occurs at layer _c_ for _c < k_ . Upon reconstruction back to layer _k_ , layer _c_ may be torn down again in the next collisions. Catastrophic forgetting resistance is proven similarly: when new samples are used for training, previous samples are not forgotten since SQANN stores the particular fingerprint _ηl[<k>]_ for each sample.

**Theorem 2** _Assume SQANN construction is completed. SQANN achieves arbitrarily high accuracy on a training dataset. Furthermore, it is resistant to catastrophic forgetting. ## 3.2 EXPERIMENT TO TEST GENERALIZABILITY OF SQANN

**Test datasets with increasing spread from training distribution** . The accuracy of SQANN on high-dimensional dataset outside the training dataset is harder to formalize in theorems. Furthermore, real life data is often noisy and possibly not regularly structured. We avoid making any related statements for SQANN for now. We instead provide empirical results on test datasets that are _similar_ to the training dataset, to the extent that each point in the test dataset is a training sample perturbed by uniform random values of increasing magnitude. We refer to the noise magnitude as the _test data spread_ . Fig. 4(A,A.2) show four domains _X_ with different test data spread. Test dataset that has larger test data spread contains data samples that are noisier and further away from the training data points. Fig. 4(B,B.2) show that SQANN naturally performs better with smaller test data spread. As the test data spread increases, larger errors are observed. Likewise, smaller spread means smaller _Ninterp_ , i.e. fewer data samples fail to activate neurons in SQANN strongly. For all, training errors are 0 _as expected from theorem 2_ .

**Classification and visualization of SQANN’s special interpretability features** . We show the use of SQANN for a simple classification problem in fig. 3(C). The ring outside is labelled 0 _._ 5, while the ring inside 1 _._ 0. With activation parameters _a_ 1 = _a_ 2 = 1, we achieve zero error not only for training dataset (to be expected from theorem 2) but also on test dataset. A special feature in SQANN is its ability to tell the user which data samples fail to activate any neurons strongly; such samples’ output must be interpolated (see SQANN propagation). In fig. 3(C) right, points marked with red open circles need interpolation. Each such point is interpolated using two training samples whose “fingerprint” neurons are most strongly excited. These two samples are shown as the two

points directly linked by colored straight lines to the open circle. This is possible because SQANN systematically stores indices of training data samples within respective layers. The list of indices organized by layers can even be explicitly printed e.g. see SQANN.ipynb, supp. materials.

**SQANN is tested for regression on Boston Housing and Diabetes Datasets** to demonstrate its generalizability to unseen/test samples, as the following (simplified here): (1) A small subset of samples _D ⊆D_ (the first 20% of full dataset _D_ ) is used to train SQANN and 9 other regression methods. (2) Mean Squared Errors (MSE) values are measured on unused data _Dtest_ on all 10 models; we expect large errors on some test samples. (3) SQANN’s activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors _eτ_ ( _x_ ) = _|SQANN_ ( _x_ ) _− y_ 0 _| > τ_ and we treat them as out-ofdistribution (OOD) samples. These samples are considered as new distinct samples to be integrated into _D_ as the new training dataset _D[′]_ . (4) Train the 10 models, now with _D[′]_ . (5) Then MSE is measured again on _Dtest_ (yes, there will be partial overfitting). From Boston dataset: for _τ_ = 5, SQANN MSE improved from 9 _._ 90 to 3 _._ 08. Decision tree improves the most with _D[′]_ (7.36 to 2.07); see table 1. For more details and diabetes dataset, see the appendix A.5.7.

## 4 CONCLUSION, LIMITATION AND FUTURE DIRECTION

**Limitations and future directions** . **TNN** is clearly limited in regards to its application to multidimensional input data although it can be useful on different types of time series data. **SQANN** limitation and possible future development currently include 1) simple sequential drawing of samples that may result in the imbalance of layer size. In the future, more sophisticated ordering of training samples can be used so that layers are constructed with meaningful and purposeful arrangement e.g. deeper layers can be purposefully reserved for rare cases; more research on this is necessary to optimize the results 2) layer destruction during the treatment of collision cases might be an inefficient mechanism, which can be improved in the future.

**Conclusion** , we have proposed TNN and SQANN, two interpretable NNs for universal approximation designed to (1) be resistant to catastrophic forgetting (2) have provably high accuracy on training datasets and (3) can be used to handle out-of-distribution samples.

## High-Level Feedback

This paper proposes two neural network constructions (TNN and SQANN) aimed at providing interpretability alongside universal approximation guarantees, with claimed benefits of catastrophic forgetting resistance and out-of-distribution detection. While the goals are laudable and the inclusion of pedagogical pencil-and-paper examples reflects a commendable attempt at clarity, the paper has several significant weaknesses that raise concerns about its suitability for a top-tier venue like ICLR. First, the technical novelty appears limited: the TNN construction essentially reduces to a hand-crafted interpolation scheme over a linearly ordered 1D dataset, which closely resembles classical constructive universal approximation results (e.g., those that already appear in textbooks like Nielsen's) and does not obviously extend to high-dimensional, realistic learning settings—the "linear ordering" assumption is highly restrictive and its generalization to multivariate inputs is not adequately motivated in the introduction. Second, the claims are somewhat overstated and imprecisely framed: "resistance to catastrophic forgetting" through a nearest-neighbor-like activation fingerprint mechanism is closer to memorization/instance-based learning than to the continual learning problem the term usually denotes, and the "interpretability" claim—identifying which training samples share activation fingerprints with a query—is essentially kernel/nearest-neighbor retrieval rebranded, without comparison to prototype networks, kNN, RBF networks, or memory-based models that already offer this property. Third, the writing and framing would benefit from tighter scholarship: the related work section dismisses prior universal approximation results as "black-box" without engaging substantively with constructive approximation literature (e.g., RBF networks, spline-based approximators, Barron's results), and key definitions (interpretability, catastrophic forgetting) are idiosyncratically redefined in ways that risk sidestepping rather than addressing the actual open problems. Finally, the clarity of exposition is uneven—notational issues appear early (e.g., $W \in \mathbb{R}^N$ rather than $\mathbb{R}^{N\times 1}$), and the paper leans heavily on appendices and supplementary material for core content, which suggests the main text may not stand on its own. Overall, the contribution feels more like a pedagogical illustration than a substantive research advance, and the significance of the claimed properties needs far stronger empirical and comparative validation.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch for W in TNN model definition
comments.quote: The model is _TNN_ ( _x_ ) = _α[T] σ_ ( _Wx_ + _b_ ) where _x ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], _α, b ∈_ R _[N]_ and _W ∈_ R _[N]_
comments.explanation: The authors define TNN(x) = α^T σ(Wx+b) with x ∈ [0,1] a scalar, and α, b ∈ R^N. For Wx to produce a vector in R^N compatible with b, W should be in R^N (as a column vector mapping scalar x to R^N), which matches. However, later in the Linear Ordering assumption the dataset is defined as {(x^(k), y^(k)) ∈ R^n × R}, implying x can be in R^n. In that case W should be in R^{N×n}, not R^N. Initially I thought this was just the scalar case, and indeed the text says 'We start with a simple scalar function'. But the linear ordering assumption explicitly writes R^n, creating an inconsistency between the scalar setup (W ∈ R^N) and the dataset dimension (R^n). This is likely an oversight where the scalar treatment (n=1) is assumed but the dataset notation wasn't updated. A careful reader could be confused about the actual input dimension supported by TNN as formally defined.

comments.title: Selective activation formula appears dimensionally/semantically wrong
comments.quote: Given selective activation _π_ ( _x, a_ ) = _a_ + _ax_[2]
comments.explanation: I tried to understand what π(x,a) = a + a*x^2 could represent as a 'selective activation.' For a selective activation that should suppress or selectively pass values, one would typically expect something like a/(a+x^2) or a/(a+ax^2), which would be near 1 when x is small and decay as x grows. The given formula a + ax^2 = a(1+x^2) grows unboundedly with x, which doesn't behave selectively at all. Combined with the Super Gaussian sg(x,a)=exp(-(x/a)^(2n)), the product or ratio should produce the 'double selective' shape with distinct peaks shown in Fig. 3(A). Given the OCR caveat, this might be a misread of something like π(x,a) = a/(a+ax^2) or a/(1+ax^2). Either way, as printed the formula cannot produce the selective behavior the text describes, and the actual equation for σ_dsa seems to be missing from the passage ('then the double selective activation is (fig. 3(A)):' with no equation following). A reader cannot reconstruct σ_dsa from what is shown.

comments.title: Missing definition of σ_dsa
comments.quote: then the _double selective activation_ is (fig. 3(A)):
comments.explanation: The sentence announces the definition of σ_dsa but the equation itself is not present in the extracted text (only a figure reference follows, then the next bolded subsection 'Nodes activation' begins). Even accounting for OCR extraction, σ_dsa is the central object of SQANN — its formula determines all subsequent constructions, the lemma's proof (which relies on σ_dsa(0)=1 and σ_dsa(||·||)<1 otherwise), and the admission/collision mechanism. Without it explicitly stated, the method is underspecified for reproduction. This is likely the location of an equation that failed to extract, but it is worth flagging because the lemma's proof implicitly assumes properties of σ_dsa (e.g., equals 1 at 0) that the reader cannot verify.

On closer reading, the layer-1 resolution is described as 'sample will simply be admitted into the layer for selective clustering' — but the admission rule (case 1) requires ∀j, [v_1^{(k)}]_j < τ_ad, which is violated if some [v_1^{(k)}]_j > τ_act = 0.9. comments.title: Lemma 1 proof uses admission conditions that don't directly preclude [v_1]_i = 1 for i ≠ j
comments.quote: Furthermore, for other _i_ = _j_ , we have [ _v_ 1] _i <_ 1 due to the admission conditions (1) and (2) used during _check N_ 1 _process_ .
comments.explanation: The lemma claims perfect retrieval via argmax. The proof asserts [v_1]_i < 1 for i ≠ j based on 'admission conditions (1) and (2).' But condition (1) is admission via weak activation (all [v_1]_j < τ_ad), and condition (2) is collision (some [v_1]_j > τ_act), where exclusively for layer 1 the colliding sample is admitted 'for selective clustering.' If selective clustering can admit two distinct x^{<j>} ≠ x^{<i>} with high mutual activation (τ_act < [v_1]_i < 1), the argmax retrieval still works because [v_1]_j = 1 dominates, which is fine. However, the text only excludes [v_1^{(k)}]_j = 1 as 'unresolvable.' The proof glosses over the selective-clustering case and does not explain why the stored x^{<j>} remains uniquely identifiable. The claim that [v_1]_i < 1 is fine (since only the exact match gives 1), but the justification 'due to admission conditions (1) and (2)' is misleading — it's really because σ_dsa(·) = 1 iff the argument is 0, a property of σ_dsa not stated in the passage. A more honest proof would appeal to σ_dsa's properties, not admission conditions.

comments.title: Definition of 'representative activations' for deeper layers is vague relative to stated procedure
comments.quote: In short, for each layer, _representative activations_ become the neurons of the layer. In layer 1, representatives activations are the samples themselves. In deeper layers, they are activations propagated to the layer.
comments.explanation: The outline states that a neuron η_k^{<j>} stores a 'fingerprint.' For layer 1, η_1^{<j>} ← x^{(k)} (the input itself). The equation [v_{k+1}]_j = σ_dsa(||v_k − η_{k+1}^{<j>}||) implies η_{k+1}^{<j>} lives in the activation space of layer k, not in input space. This is consistent with 'activations propagated to the layer,' but the layer-k construction paragraph earlier writes 'η_k^{<1>} ← v_k^{<1>}' which confirms it. However, the dimensionality of η_k depends on n_{k-1} (the size of the previous layer), which grows as neurons are admitted. This means later η_k's created after more neurons are admitted to layer k-1 would have different dimensionality than earlier ones — a potential issue the text does not address. A reader implementing this would be stuck on whether to pad, fix n_{k-1} before constructing layer k, or handle variable dimensions. This is an underspecification affecting reproducibility.

comments.title: Collision condition uses wrong layer index in notation
comments.quote: (2) collision, when there exists index _j_ at a _collided layer lc ≤ k_ such that [ _vl[<i>] c_ ] _j > τact_
comments.explanation: The passage says collision occurs when there exists j at a collided layer l_c ≤ k such that [v_{l_c}^{<i>}]_j > τ_act. This is consistent with the earlier definition. However, the activation v_{l_c}^{<i>} for l_c < k is computed using the sample u^{<i>} propagated through earlier layers. The text does not explicitly state that during layer-k construction, one re-checks activations against ALL previous layers (1..k), not just layer k. A reader might interpret 'check N_k activation' as checking only layer k. This ambiguity is important because the collision resolution depends on detecting collisions in earlier layers. The text does say 'collision could occur at any layer l_c ≤ k' but the check procedure description focuses on layer k. A careful reader could be confused about whether v_{l_c} is recomputed during layer k construction or was stored from when the sample was filtered through earlier layers.

comments.title: Collision resolution uses v_k but pushes into layer l_c
comments.quote: we destroy all layers _l > lc_ and put the collided sample into _lc_ , i.e. _Nlc → concat_ ( _Nlc , vk[<m>]_ ) and _αlc → concat_ ( _αlc , yk[<m>]_ )
comments.explanation: The collision-resolution step concatenates v_k^{<m>} (the activation at layer k) into N_{l_c} (layer l_c, where l_c ≤ k). If l_c < k, this seems inconsistent: a neuron in layer l_c should store an activation at layer l_c (a fingerprint at that layer), not an activation from a deeper layer k. The activation v_k^{<m>} lives in the space of layer-k activations, but layer l_c neurons are compared against v_{l_c-1} inputs. Adding v_k^{<m>} as a neuron in N_{l_c} would make the dimensions/semantics inconsistent. Likely the authors mean v_{l_c}^{<m>}, but as written with v_k, this is a substantive inconsistency when l_c < k. Given the summary notes l_c ≤ k and destruction of layers l > l_c, the correct object to push into layer l_c should be v_{l_c}^{<m>}, not v_k^{<m>}.

comments.title: Initialization check for v_k^{<1>} references undefined collision context
comments.quote: Initialize by first checking _vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ , the activation of _u[<]_[1] _[>]_ at layer _k_ for collision; if collision occurs, see next paragraph
comments.explanation: For the very first node being added to a newly initialized layer k (before any η_k exists), the quantity v_k^{<1>} = σ_dsa(||v_{k-1} - η_k^{<j>}||) is undefined because N_k is empty. The authors say to check for collision, which would need to refer to earlier layers l_c < k. This is workable but underspecified: a reader has to infer that the collision check here pertains to activations at earlier layers (which were already zero-or-weak-enough for the sample to be filtered down). The text never makes this explicit. A careful implementer would need clarification about what 'checking v_k^{<1>} for collision' means when layer k has no nodes yet.

comments.title: Proposition 3 completion claim is under-justified in main text
comments.quote: **Proposition 3** _SQANN construction completes with high probability p ≈_ 1
comments.explanation: The main text motivates the completion question (potential infinite collision cycles) and appeals to 'order integrity' but does not give even a sketch in the main passage. The claim 'p ≈ 1' is unusual for a deterministic construction on a fixed finite dataset — one would expect either deterministic completion or a precise probabilistic statement with explicit randomness source. The passage defers to appendix A.5.4 for the sketch and assumptions, which is acceptable, but stating 'with high probability p ≈ 1' without identifying the source of randomness (is it over data distribution? over construction order? over ties?) in the main text is a mild overclaim or at least leaves the reader unsure what probabilistic model is invoked. The assumption making p = 1 is also deferred entirely.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper proposes two neural network constructions (TNN and SQANN) aimed at providing interpretability alongside universal approximation guarantees, with claimed benefits of catastrophic forgetting resistance and out-of-distribution detection. While the goals are laudable and the inclusion of pedagogical pencil-and-paper examples reflects a commendable attempt at clarity, the paper has several significant weaknesses that raise concerns about its suitability for a top-tier venue like ICLR. First, the technical novelty appears limited: the TNN construction essentially reduces to a hand-crafted interpolation scheme over a linearly ordered 1D dataset, which closely resembles classical constructive universal approximation results (e.g., those that already appear in textbooks like Nielsen's) and does not obviously extend to high-dimensional, realistic learning settings—the "linear ordering" assumption is highly restrictive and its generalization to multivariate inputs is not adequately motivated in the introduction. Second, the claims are somewhat overstated and imprecisely framed: "resistance to catastrophic forgetting" through a nearest-neighbor-like activation fingerprint mechanism is closer to memorization/instance-based learning than to the continual learning problem the term usually denotes, and the "interpretability" claim—identifying which training samples share activation fingerprints with a query—is essentially kernel/nearest-neighbor retrieval rebranded, without comparison to prototype networks, kNN, RBF networks, or memory-based models that already offer this property. Third, the writing and framing would benefit from tighter scholarship: the related work section dismisses prior universal approximation results as "black-box" without engaging substantively with constructive approximation literature (e.g., RBF networks, spline-based approximators, Barron's results), and key definitions (interpretability, catastrophic forgetting) are idiosyncratically redefined in ways that risk sidestepping rather than addressing the actual open problems. Finally, the clarity of exposition is uneven—notational issues appear early (e.g., $W \in \mathbb{R}^N$ rather than $\mathbb{R}^{N\times 1}$), and the paper leans heavily on appendices and supplementary material for core content, which suggests the main text may not stand on its own. Overall, the contribution feels more like a pedagogical illustration than a substantive research advance, and the significance of the claimed properties needs far stronger empirical and comparative validation.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch for W in TNN model definition
comments.quote: The model is _TNN_ ( _x_ ) = _α[T] σ_ ( _Wx_ + _b_ ) where _x ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], _α, b ∈_ R _[N]_ and _W ∈_ R _[N]_
comments.explanation: The authors define TNN(x) = α^T σ(Wx+b) with x ∈ [0,1] a scalar, and α, b ∈ R^N. For Wx to produce a vector in R^N compatible with b, W should be in R^N (as a column vector mapping scalar x to R^N), which matches. However, later in the Linear Ordering assumption the dataset is defined as {(x^(k), y^(k)) ∈ R^n × R}, implying x can be in R^n. In that case W should be in R^{N×n}, not R^N. Initially I thought this was just the scalar case, and indeed the text says 'We start with a simple scalar function'. But the linear ordering assumption explicitly writes R^n, creating an inconsistency between the scalar setup (W ∈ R^N) and the dataset dimension (R^n). This is likely an oversight where the scalar treatment (n=1) is assumed but the dataset notation wasn't updated. A careful reader could be confused about the actual input dimension supported by TNN as formally defined.

comments.title: Dummy value formula appears malformed/unclear
comments.quote: For ∆[(] _[N]_[)] , we need a 1 dummy _x_[(] _[N]_[+1)] value or we can directly choose its value, e.g. _N_[Σ] _k[N]_ =1 _[−]_[1][∆][(] _[k]_[)][.
comments.explanation: The text gives an example formula for choosing Δ^(N) directly. After OCR, this reads like (1/N)·Σ_{k=1}^{N-1} Δ^(k), i.e., the average of the existing spacings, which is a sensible default. I think this is just OCR corruption of a reasonable formula rather than a genuine error, and the authors also note the chosen value only affects the graph's left edge. Flagging as minor because it may cause brief confusion but the intent is recoverable.

comments.title: Ordered activation indexing inconsistency with TNN construction
comments.quote: Consider first ( _Wx_[(2)] + _b_ ) _N_ = _−a_ and ( _Wx_[(1)] + _b_ ) _N_ = _a_ and solve them. This yields _WN_ = 2 _a/_ ∆[(1)] and _bN_ = _a − WN x_[(1)] where ∆[(1)] = _x_[(1)] _− x_[(2)]
comments.explanation: I first tried to verify that this setup yields the target ordered activations. The paper earlier states $\sigma(Wx^{(1)}+b) = [1,1,\dots,1]^T$ and $\sigma(Wx^{(2)}+b) = [1,\dots,1,0]^T$. That means the N-th component of $\sigma(Wx^{(1)}+b)$ is 1 (pre-activation large positive, i.e. $+a$), while the N-th component of $\sigma(Wx^{(2)}+b)$ is 0 (pre-activation large negative, i.e. $-a$). So the equations $(Wx^{(1)}+b)_N = a$ and $(Wx^{(2)}+b)_N = -a$ are consistent. Solving: $W_N(x^{(1)} - x^{(2)}) = 2a$, so $W_N = 2a/\Delta^{(1)}$ with $\Delta^{(1)} = x^{(1)} - x^{(2)}$. This is correct given the assumption $x^{(1)} > x^{(2)}$. Actually this is consistent with the linear ordering assumption. No error here — I initially worried about sign but it checks out.

comments.title: Pencil-and-paper example: sign/coefficient of TNN formula does not match construction
comments.explanation:
Let me verify this pencil-and-paper example by applying the construction. Under the linear ordering assumption, we must have $x^{(N)} < \dots < x^{(1)}$, so $x^{(1)}=1, x^{(2)}=0.5, x^{(3)}=0$ with $y^{(1)}=1, y^{(2)}=2, y^{(3)}=3$. Then $\Delta^{(1)} = 0.5, \Delta^{(2)} = 0.5$. With $a=5$: $W_N = W_3 = 2(5)/0.5 = 20$, $b_3 = a - W_3 x^{(1)} = 5 - 20(1) = -15$. Similarly $W_2 = 20$, $b_2 = 5 - 20(0.5) = -5$. For $k=1$ (the leftmost neuron), we need a dummy $x^{(N+1)}=x^{(4)}$; if chosen so $\Delta^{(3)}$ gives $W_1 = 20$, then $b_1 = 5 - 20(0) = 5$. So the pre-activations are $20x+5, 20x-5, 20x-15$. 

Now for $\alpha$: the construction gives $\alpha = A^{-1}y$ where $A^{-1}$ has 1 on diagonal, $-1$ on superdiagonal. With $y = [y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}, y^{(3)}]^T = [1,2,3]^T$, we get $\alpha_1 = 1-2 = -1$, $\alpha_2 = 2-3 = -1$, $\alpha_3 = 3$. So $TNN(x) = -\sigma(20x+5) - \sigma(20x-5) + 3\sigma(20x-15)$.

The paper states $TNN(x) = 3\sigma(20x+5) - \sigma(20x-5) - \sigma(20x-15)$. This has the coefficient 3 on the leftmost term (which in the ordering corresponds to the lowest-$x$ neuron activated first), and the signs/coefficients don't match my derivation. Checking by substitution: at $x=1$ (should give $y=1$), with large activations $\sigma(25), \sigma(15), \sigma(5)$ all ≈ 1, the paper's formula gives $3-1-1 = 1$. ✓. At $x=0.5$ (should give $y=2$): $\sigma(15)\approx 1, \sigma(5)\approx 1, \sigma(-5)\approx 0$, gives $3-1-0 = 2$. ✓. At $x=0$ (should give $y=3$): $\sigma(5)\approx 1, \sigma(-5)\approx 0, \sigma(-15)\approx 0$, gives $3-0-0 = 3$. ✓.

So the paper's formula is numerically correct! The issue is in my understanding of the indexing. It appears the paper indexes $\alpha$ such that $\alpha_1$ corresponds to the neuron activated by all samples (the leftmost, lowest-$x$ neuron), with coefficient 3. But earlier the text writes $y^{(1)} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i\sigma(Wx^{(1)}+b)_i$ and $y^{(N)} = \alpha_1\sigma(Wx^{(N)}+b)_1$, which means $\alpha_1$ is the coefficient for the neuron activated by ALL samples (including $x^{(N)}$), i.e., the 'always-on' neuron. That's consistent with the pencil example: the $20x+5$ neuron (with $b_1=5$) is always active for $x\in[0,1]$. Then $\alpha_1 = y^{(N)} = 3$, $\alpha_2 = y^{(N-1)}-y^{(N)} = 2-3 = -1$, $\alpha_3 = y^{(1)}-y^{(2)} = 1-2 = -1$. This matches!

So the pencil example is actually correct — my initial derivation of $\alpha = A^{-1}y$ got the indexing backward. The passage is self-consistent. No issue.

comments.title: Selective activation formula appears dimensionally/semantically wrong
comments.quote: Given selective activation _π_ ( _x, a_ ) = _a_ + _ax_[2]
comments.explanation: I tried to understand what π(x,a) = a + a*x^2 could represent as a 'selective activation.' For a selective activation that should suppress or selectively pass values, one would typically expect something like a/(a+x^2) or a/(a+ax^2), which would be near 1 when x is small and decay as x grows. The given formula a + ax^2 = a(1+x^2) grows unboundedly with x, which doesn't behave selectively at all. Combined with the Super Gaussian sg(x,a)=exp(-(x/a)^(2n)), the product or ratio should produce the 'double selective' shape with distinct peaks shown in Fig. 3(A). Given the OCR caveat, this might be a misread of something like π(x,a) = a/(a+ax^2) or a/(1+ax^2). Either way, as printed the formula cannot produce the selective behavior the text describes, and the actual equation for σ_dsa seems to be missing from the passage ('then the double selective activation is (fig. 3(A)):' with no equation following). A reader cannot reconstruct σ_dsa from what is shown.

comments.title: Missing definition of σ_dsa
comments.quote: then the _double selective activation_ is (fig. 3(A)):
comments.explanation: The sentence announces the definition of σ_dsa but the equation itself is not present in the extracted text (only a figure reference follows, then the next bolded subsection 'Nodes activation' begins). Even accounting for OCR extraction, σ_dsa is the central object of SQANN — its formula determines all subsequent constructions, the lemma's proof (which relies on σ_dsa(0)=1 and σ_dsa(||·||)<1 otherwise), and the admission/collision mechanism. Without it explicitly stated, the method is underspecified for reproduction. This is likely the location of an equation that failed to extract, but it is worth flagging because the lemma's proof implicitly assumes properties of σ_dsa (e.g., equals 1 at 0) that the reader cannot verify.

However, the text only excludes [v_1^{(k)}]_j = 1 as 'unresolvable.' The proof glosses over the selective-clustering case and does not explain why the stored x^{<j>} remains uniquely identifiable. However, the dimensionality of η_k depends on n_{k-1} (the size of the previous layer), which grows as neurons are admitted. This means later η_k's created after more neurons are admitted to layer k-1 would have different dimensionality than earlier ones — a potential issue the text does not address. A reader implementing this would be stuck on whether to pad, fix n_{k-1} before constructing layer k, or handle variable dimensions. This is an underspecification affecting reproducibility.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes potentially interesting constructive neural-network schemes aimed at interpretability and memorization-style universal approximation, but in its current form the quality and clarity are not sufficient for a top-tier venue. The central claims—universal approximation “more general” than classical results, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, fine-grained interpretability, and out-of-distribution detection—are stated very broadly but are not yet convincingly defined, motivated, or supported in the opening sections. Much of the construction appears to rely on strong and restrictive assumptions, especially a linearly ordered dataset and essentially sample-specific activation patterns, which risks reducing the contribution to interpolation or memorization rather than a broadly useful approximation framework. The interpretability argument is also underdeveloped: assigning neurons to ordered samples or signal segments may make the model transparent in simple one-dimensional settings, but it is unclear how this scales to high-dimensional data or provides meaningful explanations beyond nearest-neighbor-like activation fingerprints. The discussion of catastrophic forgetting similarly seems potentially trivial if new data can be incorporated by expanding or modifying the architecture without affecting old activations, and the OOD claim based on “weak activations” requires much more formalization and empirical validation. Clarity is a major issue: notation is frequently hard to parse, definitions are informal, several claims are overextended, and the writing contains grammatical and formatting problems that obscure the technical content. To strengthen the paper, the authors should sharply delimit the scope of the theory, provide precise theorems and assumptions in the main text, compare against simpler baselines such as lookup tables, kernel methods, RBF networks, splines, and nearest-neighbor models, and demonstrate that the proposed constructions offer practical interpretability or generalization advantages beyond controlled toy examples.

comments.title: Stated inverse for the triangular activation matrix appears inconsistent with the sample ordering
comments.quote: Putting them together as _y_ = [ _y_[(1)] _, . . . , y_[(] _[N]_[)] ] _[T]_ , we get _y_ = _Aα_ where _A_ is an upper-left triangular matrix and the inverse _A[−]_[1] exists. Thus, _α_ = _A[−]_[1] _y_ , a matrix such that _A[−] ij_[1][= 1][ along the diagonal,] _[ A] i,i[−]_[1] +1[=] _[ −]_[1][ and zeroes otherwise, which facilitates a convenient] computation.
comments.explanation: The surrounding construction suggests that the inverse is intended to have 1s on the diagonal and -1s on one adjacent diagonal. But with the written activation ordering, x^(1) activates all neurons, x^(2) activates all but the last, ..., and x^(N) activates only the first, the matrix A has rows like [1,1,...,1], [1,1,...,0], ..., [1,0,...,0]. For N=3 this gives alpha = [y^(3), y^(2)-y^(3), y^(1)-y^(2)]^T, not the straightforward forward-difference formula suggested by the stated inverse. The later example appears to use the reversed-difference version, so the construction may be inferable, but the stated inverse/indexing is misleading and could cause an implementation to assign alpha values to the wrong neurons.

comments.title: Mid-point property is stated as exact although sigmoid saturation only gives it approximately for finite a
comments.explanation: The midpoint construction makes the relevant neuron have preactivation 0 and sigmoid value 1/2. However, for the other neurons, a standard sigmoid at finite a is not exactly 0 or 1; it is only close when a is large. Earlier the paper acknowledges that the desired ordered activation pattern is approximate for large a and exact only as a → ∞. The midpoint equality should therefore be qualified as asymptotic or idealized unless the proof uses a hard-threshold activation rather than a sigmoid.

comments.title: Claim of “universal approximation” is stronger than the stated finite-training-set theorem
comments.quote:
While Nielsen (2015) provides only visual demonstration, the following result shows rigorous proof on universal approximation at work (python code also available).

**Theorem 1** _TNN achieves arbitrarily high accuracy on the training dataset. Proof: see appendix A.4.1. Also see example results in fig. 2._
comments.explanation: The theorem as stated proves arbitrarily high accuracy on a finite training dataset. That is a useful interpolation result, but it is weaker than standard universal approximation, which usually means density in a function space, such as uniform approximation on compact sets. Even under the paper’s broader framing, fitting finite samples does not by itself establish approximation between samples or on a domain without additional assumptions. The phrase “rigorous proof on universal approximation” therefore overstates what Theorem 1 proves.

comments.title: Catastrophic-forgetting claim follows only from refitting the augmented dataset, not from a continual-learning mechanism
comments.quote: Catastrophic forgetting is not a problem during re-training because when ood samples from the test dataset are included as training points, each training point _x ∈ D_ is still identified with a neuron. By theorem 1, _x_ is still approximated accurately.
comments.explanation: The construction can fit an augmented finite dataset, so adding new samples and rebuilding or refitting can preserve training accuracy on the old samples. But that is weaker than the usual continual-learning claim that catastrophic forgetting is avoided. Theorem 1 does not show that the existing model can be updated without access to old data, without full recomputation, without changing old representations, or with controlled finite-a error. Since ordering and alpha values can change when new points are inserted, the claim should be framed as an existence/refitting result for D ∪ A rather than as a general resistance-to-forgetting mechanism.

comments.title: Proposition 1 is underspecified and can become trivial depending on how A is chosen
comments.quote: **Proposition 1** _**Errors on monotonous interval** . Given finite training, test datasets D, D[′] , there exists A ⊆ D[′] such that, using TNN constructed with D ∪A, for all samples in test dataset_ ( _x[′] , y[′]_ ) _∈ D[′] , sample-wise error e_ = _|y[′] − TNN_ ( _x[′]_ ) _| has an upper bound max_ ( _|y[′] − y_[(] _[k]_[+1)] _|, |y[′] − y_[(] _[k]_[)] _|_ ) _for some k. Proof: see appendix A.4.2._
comments.explanation: The intended interpolation idea is plausible: between neighboring training samples, the prediction error at a test point can be bounded by its distance from the endpoint labels. But the statement does not clarify how A is selected. If A can equal all of D′, then every test sample can be made a training sample, making the result essentially trivial. The phrase “for some k” is also unclear in the augmented ordering, and the title refers to a “monotonous interval” although the displayed proposition states no monotonicity or interval condition. As written, the proposition does not clearly support the prose claim that similar test datasets will yield small error without additional assumptions.

comments.title: SQANN generalization and nearest-neighbor-avoidance claims are not supported by the stated mechanism
comments.quote:
Since SQANN incorporates multi-layer structure, it avoids being a non-generalizing model that nearest neighbours methods suffer from; see the remark in (Pedregosa et al. (2011a)).

We create the SQANN model that predicts _y_[(] _[k]_[)] = _SQANN_ ( _x_[(] _[k]_[)] ) with provably perfect accuracy and generalizes well to similar test distribution.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that SQANN generalizes well to similar test distributions and avoids nearest-neighbor non-generalization because it has a multilayer structure. But depth alone does not imply generalization, and nearest-neighbor methods are not categorically non-generalizing under all assumptions. The passage gives admission, collision, and filtering rules, but it does not formalize “similar test distribution” or provide a theorem linking the multilayer mechanism to generalization. The claim should either be qualified as empirical intuition or tied to precise assumptions and guarantees.
