comments.quote: To do this, take a new sample ( _x_[(] _[k]_[)] _, y_[(] _[k]_[)] ) _, k >_ 2 and we _check N_ 1 _activation_ , i.e. let _v_ 1[(] _[k]_[)] be the activation of current layer by this new sample... Then, either: (1) new sample is _admitted to N_ 1 as a new distinct node/neuron. If for all _j_ such that [ _v_ 1[(2)][]] _[j][< τ][ad]_[, then] _N_ 1 _→ concat_ ( _N_ 1 _, x_[(] _[k]_[)] ) and _α_ 1 _→ concat_ ( _α_ 1 _, y_[(] _[k]_[)] ).
comments.explanation: The intended algorithm seems to initialize layer 1 with the first sample, then iterate over the remaining samples. But the text says k > 2, which would leave the second sample unprocessed unless it is handled elsewhere. The admission condition also uses v_1^(2) while the rest of the paragraph refers to the current sample k. If taken literally, this changes the algorithm and makes reproduction ambiguous. The text should consistently use the current sample’s activation and explain whether iteration starts at k = 2 or k = 3.

comments.title: Collision, selective clustering, and unused-sample bookkeeping are not specified enough to reproduce SQANN
comments.quote:
Collision . A sample activates one or more neurons strongly i.e. _∃j, k,_ [ _vk_ ] _j > τact_ . The earliest layer where collision occurs is denoted _lc_ . Such sample is integrated into _lc_ , thus very similar samples are _selectively clustered_ .

Assume every layer _l ∈_ Λ = _{_ 1 _, . . . , k −_ 1 _}_ have been constructed using _Xi∈_ Λ _⊆ X_ . Assume there are still unused data samples i.e. _U_ = _X \ ..._
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is that weak activations create new nodes, strong activations indicate similarity to existing nodes, and intermediate cases are filtered to deeper layers. But the operational rule for “integrated into l_c” is not defined: it is unclear whether a new node is appended, an existing representative is updated, how multiple activated nodes are handled, how labels are combined, or what numerical rule defines sufficiently similar labels. In addition, the sets/lists X_i and U are introduced only implicitly, even though reconstruction and termination depend on tracking which samples have been used in which layers and in what order. The construction needs precise definitions of these objects and update rules.

comments.title: Proof of Lemma 1 relies on an unstated uniqueness or tie-handling condition
comments.quote: Furthermore, for other _i_ = _j_ , we have [ _v_ 1] _i <_ 1 due to the admission conditions (1) and (2) used during _check N_ 1 _process_ . Finally, computing _y_ = _α_ 1 _[<j>]_ where _j_ = _argmaxj′_ [ _v_ 1] _j′_ , we retrieve the exact value.
comments.explanation: The proof intends to show that a stored training sample activates its own node maximally, so argmax retrieves the correct label. Interpreting the apparent “i = j” as “i ≠ j,” the argument still requires an assumption that no other node also achieves the same maximum activation, or a tie-breaking rule that preserves the label. Duplicate identical inputs with the same label are not ill-defined for supervised learning but could create argmax ties unless collapsed. Without a uniqueness/no-tie condition, the final argmax step does not by itself guarantee exact retrieval.

comments.title: Deeper-layer construction and collision resolution use vectors from inconsistent layer spaces
comments.quote:
Initialize by first checking _vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ , the activation of _u[<]_[1] _[>]_ at layer _k_ for collision; if collision occurs, see next paragraph, otherwise, set _Nk_ = ( _ηk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) _, αk_ = ( _yk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) i.e. _ηk[<]_[1] _[>] ← vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ .

We destroy all layers _l > lc_ and put the collided sample into _lc_ , i.e. _Nlc → concat_ ( _Nlc , vk[<m>]_ ) and _αlc → concat_ ( _αlc , yk[<m>]_ ) (push-node in the pseudo code).
comments.explanation: The prediction rule computes layer-k activations from the previous layer’s activation vector, so a node in layer k should live in the same space as the input fingerprint to layer k, apparently v_{k-1}. But the construction says η_k is assigned from v_k, which is circular if v_k is the output of layer k. Similarly, when a sample collides at an earlier layer l_c < k, the text appends v_k into N_{l_c}, even though nodes in l_c should be compared against the layer-l_c input/fingerprint, not a later-layer vector. These layer-space inconsistencies affect the core algorithm and need clarification or correction.

comments.title: Interpolation rule is not reproducibly specified
comments.quote: Suppose _V_ 1 = [ _vm_ ] _i_ and _V_ 2 = [ _vn_ ] _j_ are the two most activated neurons, then the interpolated value can be, for example, _y_ = _V_ 1[ _αmV_ ]1 _i_ ++ _VV_ 22[ _αn_ ] _j_ .
comments.explanation: The displayed expression is visibly corrupted, but even assuming the intended formula is a weighted average, key details remain unspecified. It is unclear whether the two most activated neurons are chosen globally across layers or only within the final layer, how activations from different layers are compared, how ties or zero weights are handled, and how the rule applies when Y is categorical rather than a vector space supporting averages. Since this interpolation governs predictions on non-training inputs, the method is not reproducible from the text alone.

comments.title: Completion claim uses an undefined probability statement
comments.quote: **Proposition 3** _SQANN construction completes with high probability p ≈_ 1 _. See appendix A.5.4 for (1) sketch of proof and a required assumption (2) stronger assumption needed for p_ = 1 _._
comments.explanation: Because collisions can destroy later layers and trigger reconstruction, termination is a real concern. But the statement “with high probability p ≈ 1” is not mathematically specified in the main text. It does not say what is random: the sample distribution, sample ordering, perturbations, activation parameters, or tie-breaking. Since Theorem 2 depends on construction completing, the proposition should state the probability space and assumptions under which cycling has small or zero probability.

comments.title: Evaluation protocol leaks test labels into retraining while presenting the result as generalization to unseen/OOD samples
comments.quote: **SQANN is tested for regression on Boston Housing and Diabetes Datasets** to demonstrate its generalizability to unseen/test samples... (3) SQANN’s activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors _eτ_ ( _x_ ) = _|SQANN_ ( _x_ ) _− y_ 0 _| > τ_ and we treat them as out-ofdistribution (OOD) samples. These samples are considered as new distinct samples to be integrated into _D_ as the new training dataset _D[′]_ . (4) Train the 10 models, now with _D[′]_ . (5) Then MSE is measured again on _Dtest_ (yes, there will be partial overfitting).
comments.explanation: The experiment selects difficult samples from Dtest using their true labels, adds them to the training set, retrains, and then evaluates again on the same Dtest. After retraining, part of the evaluation set is no longer unseen, so the post-integration MSE mixes performance on held-out points with performance on points incorporated into training. In addition, the OOD criterion is a ground-truth error threshold, not an activation-only detector; large prediction error is not equivalent to being out of distribution. The experiment may illustrate active learning, transductive incorporation, or hard-example retraining, but it does not cleanly demonstrate generalization to unseen OOD samples unless results are separately reported on the remaining held-out data.

comments.title: Reported Boston Housing improvement claims are inconsistent with the table and wording
comments.quote: From Boston dataset: for _τ_ = 5, SQANN MSE improved from 9 _._ 90 to 3 _._ 08. Decision tree improves the most with _D[′]_ (7.36 to 2.07); see table 1.
comments.explanation: The table appears to show SQANN moving from about 9.898 to about 7.998 for the e5/τ=5 row, while the 3.076 value belongs to the e2/τ=2 row. Similarly, the decision-tree value 2.072 appears to come from the e2 row rather than the e5 row. Also, if “improves the most” means largest MSE reduction, the table does not support it; for example, the linear model has a much larger absolute drop. If the intended claim is that the decision tree obtains the best final e2 MSE, the wording should say that.

comments.title: SQANN/SQANN′ model-selection rule is underspecified and affects the reported comparison
comments.quote: For SQANN, the initial model trained on _D_ is kept after SQANN’ is trained on _D[′]_ . Thus, we can choose results based on the strength of activations between SQANN and SQANN’.
comments.explanation: Keeping both the original and updated SQANN may be part of the proposed forgetting-resistance mechanism, but the experimental selection rule is not defined. It is unclear whether the choice between SQANN and SQANN′ is made per test sample or globally, what scalar activation strength is used, how ties or interpolation cases are handled, and whether the rule was fixed before evaluation. Since this can materially change the reported MSE, the comparison is hard to interpret without a reproducible selection procedure.

comments.title: Unseeded randomness undermines exact reproducibility of reported numerical results
comments.quote: Results are easily reproducible even with without random number seeding since data distributions are sufficiently controlled.
comments.explanation: Controlled distributions may make qualitative behavior robust, and accompanying notebooks may help reproduction. But if tables or figures depend on randomly sampled train/test splits, perturbations, noise, sample orderings, or generated datasets, then rerunning without fixed seeds can produce different numerical values. The claim should distinguish exact reproducibility of reported numbers from qualitative robustness across random draws, or provide fixed seeds/generated datasets.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper proposes potentially interesting constructive neural-network schemes aimed at interpretability and memorization-style universal approximation, but in its current form the quality and clarity are not sufficient for a top-tier venue. The central claims—universal approximation “more general” than classical results, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, fine-grained interpretability, and out-of-distribution detection—are stated very broadly but are not yet convincingly defined, motivated, or supported in the opening sections. Much of the construction appears to rely on strong and restrictive assumptions, especially a linearly ordered dataset and essentially sample-specific activation patterns, which risks reducing the contribution to interpolation or memorization rather than a broadly useful approximation framework. The interpretability argument is also underdeveloped: assigning neurons to ordered samples or signal segments may make the model transparent in simple one-dimensional settings, but it is unclear how this scales to high-dimensional data or provides meaningful explanations beyond nearest-neighbor-like activation fingerprints. The discussion of catastrophic forgetting similarly seems potentially trivial if new data can be incorporated by expanding or modifying the architecture without affecting old activations, and the OOD claim based on “weak activations” requires much more formalization and empirical validation. Clarity is a major issue: notation is frequently hard to parse, definitions are informal, several claims are overextended, and the writing contains grammatical and formatting problems that obscure the technical content. To strengthen the paper, the authors should sharply delimit the scope of the theory, provide precise theorems and assumptions in the main text, compare against simpler baselines such as lookup tables, kernel methods, RBF networks, splines, and nearest-neighbor models, and demonstrate that the proposed constructions offer practical interpretability or generalization advantages beyond controlled toy examples.

comments.title: Stated inverse for the triangular activation matrix appears inconsistent with the sample ordering
comments.quote: Putting them together as _y_ = [ _y_[(1)] _, . . . , y_[(] _[N]_[)] ] _[T]_ , we get _y_ = _Aα_ where _A_ is an upper-left triangular matrix and the inverse _A[−]_[1] exists. Thus, _α_ = _A[−]_[1] _y_ , a matrix such that _A[−] ij_[1][= 1][ along the diagonal,] _[ A] i,i[−]_[1] +1[=] _[ −]_[1][ and zeroes otherwise, which facilitates a convenient] computation.
comments.explanation: I initially suspected this might be an OCR problem because the displayed matrix formula is garbled. However, the surrounding text makes the intended claim fairly clear: the authors seem to be saying that the inverse has 1s on the diagonal and -1s on one adjacent diagonal. With the activation ordering stated just above, namely x^(1) activates all neurons, x^(2) activates all but the last, ..., and x^(N) activates only the first, the matrix A for y = A alpha has rows like [1,1,...,1], [1,1,...,0], ..., [1,0,...,0], assuming y is ordered as [y^(1),...,y^(N)]^T as written. For N=3, this gives alpha = [y^(3), y^(2)-y^(3), y^(1)-y^(2)]^T, not alpha_i = y_i - y_{i+1} with alpha_N = y_N. The pencil-and-paper example later actually uses the reversed-difference version, so the construction itself may be understood by example, but the stated inverse is misleading or wrong under the written indexing. This is not just cosmetic: a reader implementing the formula as written would assign the alpha values to the wrong neurons unless they infer an unstated reversal of indices.

comments.title: Mid-point property is stated as exact although sigmoid saturation only gives it approximately for finite a
comments.explanation: The construction correctly sets the relevant preactivation to +a at x^(k) and -a at x^(k+1), so at the midpoint that particular neuron has preactivation 0 and sigmoid value 1/2. My concern is with the other neurons: for a standard sigmoid and finite a, their activations are not exactly 0 or 1, only close to those values when a is large. Earlier the authors explicitly acknowledge that the desired ordered activation is only approximate for large a and exact only as a -> infinity. Under that same convention, the midpoint equality should also be qualified as an asymptotic or idealized statement unless the proof uses a hard-threshold activation rather than sigmoid. As written, a careful reader could incorrectly conclude that the equality holds exactly for the finite-a TNN used in the examples.

comments.title: Claim of “universal approximation” is stronger than the stated theorem on finite training accuracy
comments.quote:
While Nielsen (2015) provides only visual demonstration, the following result shows rigorous proof on universal approximation at work (python code also available).

**Theorem 1** _TNN achieves arbitrarily high accuracy on the training dataset. Proof: see appendix A.4.1. Also see example results in fig. 2._
comments.explanation: I understand that the paper uses a broader, somewhat nonstandard notion of universal approximation introduced earlier, and that exact or arbitrarily accurate interpolation on arbitrary finite datasets is a useful ingredient. Still, the theorem stated here is only a finite-sample training-accuracy result. In standard approximation theory, universal approximation normally means density in a function space, e.g. uniform approximation on compact sets under appropriate assumptions. Even under the authors' broader framing, fitting a finite training set does not by itself establish approximation between samples or on a domain without additional sampling/interpolation assumptions. The phrase “rigorous proof on universal approximation at work” therefore overstates what Theorem 1, as stated, proves.

comments.title: Catastrophic-forgetting claim follows only from reconstructing/refitting the augmented dataset, not from a demonstrated continual-learning mechanism
comments.quote: Catastrophic forgetting is not a problem during re-training because when ood samples from the test dataset are included as training points, each training point _x ∈ D_ is still identified with a neuron. By theorem 1, _x_ is still approximated accurately.
comments.explanation: The authors' intended point is understandable: because the TNN construction can fit any finite ordered dataset to high training accuracy, adding new out-of-distribution samples to the training set and reconstructing the model can preserve the old samples as well. That does establish a kind of existence result for an expanded architecture. However, it is weaker than the usual continual-learning claim suggested by “catastrophic forgetting is not a problem.” Theorem 1 guarantees high accuracy on the augmented finite training set after construction; it does not show that the existing model M can be updated without access to old data, without full recomputation, without changing the old representation, or with controlled numerical error for finite a. Also, in TNN the ordering and alpha values generally change when new points are inserted, so old samples are not necessarily preserved by the same neuron in a literal sense. The claim would be more accurate if framed as “refitting on D ∪ A can preserve training accuracy on D by construction,” rather than as a general resistance-to-forgetting result.

comments.title: Proposition 1 risks being vacuous or underspecified without clarifying how A and k are chosen
comments.quote: **Proposition 1** _**Errors on monotonous interval** . Given finite training, test datasets D, D[′] , there exists A ⊆ D[′] such that, using TNN constructed with D ∪A, for all samples in test dataset_ ( _x[′] , y[′]_ ) _∈ D[′] , sample-wise error e_ = _|y[′] − TNN_ ( _x[′]_ ) _| has an upper bound max_ ( _|y[′] − y_[(] _[k]_[+1)] _|, |y[′] − y_[(] _[k]_[)] _|_ ) _for some k. Proof: see appendix A.4.2._
comments.explanation: I can see the likely idea: between two neighboring training samples, the TNN output should interpolate between the two endpoint labels, so the prediction error at a test point is bounded by the larger distance from the test label to the two endpoint labels. That is a reasonable local interpolation statement under the idealized/saturated activation behavior. What remains unclear in the proposition as written is the role of A. Since A may be any subset of D′, taking A = D′ would make all test samples training samples and make the result essentially trivial. If the intended result is nontrivial out-of-distribution incorporation, the rule for selecting A and the meaning of “for some k” in the augmented ordering need to be explicit. The title also says “monotonous interval,” but no monotonicity or interval condition appears in the displayed statement. As a result, the proposition does not clearly support the preceding prose claim that resembling test datasets will yield small error, except under additional assumptions not stated here.

comments.title: Multi-layer SQANN structure is asserted to avoid nearest-neighbor non-generalization without support in the passage
comments.quote: Since SQANN incorporates multi-layer structure, it avoids being a non-generalizing model that nearest neighbours methods suffer from; see the remark in (Pedregosa et al. (2011a)).
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to distinguish SQANN from a pure nearest-neighbor lookup, and the subsequent construction may indeed add filtering and interpolation behavior. However, the statement as written is too strong: simply having multiple layers does not by itself imply generalization, nor does it automatically avoid the well-known limitations of nearest-neighbor methods. Conversely, nearest-neighbor methods do have standard consistency/generalization results under suitable assumptions, so describing them categorically as “non-generalizing” is also imprecise. This is a framing issue rather than a formula error, but the claim should be tied to a specific mechanism or theorem rather than inferred from depth alone.

comments.title: Ordered data are introduced using unordered set notation
comments.quote: The order of data sample within the dataset matters, thus we define our own indexing to prevent confusion. Let the finite training set be _{_ ( _x_[(] _[k]_[)] _, y_[(] _[k]_[)] ) _∈ X × Y_ : _k_ = 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . , N }_ .
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: SQANN construction is sequential, and the relabelling/indexing conventions are meant to make that sequence explicit. However, the displayed object is a set, which is unordered by standard mathematical convention. This is not just cosmetic here, because later admission, collision, and layer assignment depend on the order in which samples are processed. A careful reader could reasonably wonder whether different enumerations of the same set produce different SQANNs, and whether the claimed guarantees hold for all orders or only for the chosen sequence. This would be resolved by defining the training data as an ordered tuple/list, or by explicitly stating that the braces denote an indexed collection with a fixed externally supplied order.

comments.title: Generalization claim is stated before a formal condition or guarantee is given
comments.quote: We create the SQANN model that predicts _y_[(] _[k]_[)] = _SQANN_ ( _x_[(] _[k]_[)] ) with provably perfect accuracy and generalizes well to similar test distribution.
comments.explanation: The perfect-training-accuracy part is consistent with the paper's stated objective and is later supported, at least for layer 1 in the shown lemma and presumably for the full SQANN later. The phrase about generalizing well to a 'similar test distribution' is more problematic. I looked for whether 'similar' is formalized in the surrounding SQANN construction, but the passage only gives activation thresholds, collision/admission rules, and an informal notion of selective clustering. Without a metric/distributional assumption and a theorem or reproducible evaluation protocol, this reads as a stronger claim than the construction has established. It could be made defensible by qualifying it as an empirical expectation or by stating the precise assumptions under which generalization is guaranteed.

comments.title: Layer-1 construction appears to skip or misindex the second training sample
comments.quote: To do this, take a new sample ( _x_[(] _[k]_[)] _, y_[(] _[k]_[)] ) _, k >_ 2 and we _check N_ 1 _activation_ , i.e. let _v_ 1[(] _[k]_[)] be the activation of current layer by this new sample, i.e. [ _v_ 1[(] _[k]_[)][]] _[j]_[=] _[ σ][dsa]_  _||x_[(] _[k]_[)] _− η_ 1 _[<j>] ||_  for all _η_ 1 _[<j>] ∈ N_ 1. Then, either: (1) new sample is _admitted to N_ 1 as a new distinct node/neuron. If for all _j_ such that [ _v_ 1[(2)][]] _[j][< τ][ad]_[, then] _N_ 1 _→ concat_ ( _N_ 1 _, x_[(] _[k]_[)] ) and _α_ 1 _→ concat_ ( _α_ 1 _, y_[(] _[k]_[)] ).
comments.explanation: The intended algorithm is clear enough: initialize layer 1 with the first sample, then iterate over the remaining samples and decide admission/collision/filtering from their activations. But as written, the iteration starts with `k > 2`, which would leave the second sample unprocessed unless it is handled elsewhere. In the same paragraph, the admission test uses `[v_1^{(2)}]_j` while the surrounding text and concatenation use the generic sample index `k`. This may be a typographical or OCR issue, but if taken literally it changes the algorithm and makes reproduction ambiguous. The text should consistently use `k \ge 2` or explain where sample 2 is processed, and the admission condition should use the activation of the current sample.

comments.title: Collision/selective-clustering update is not specified enough to reproduce
comments.quote: Collision . A sample activates one or more neurons strongly i.e. _∃j, k,_ [ _vk_ ] _j > τact_ . The earliest layer where collision occurs is denoted _lc_ . Such sample is integrated into _lc_ , thus very similar samples are _selectively clustered_ .
comments.explanation: I can see the high-level design: weak activations create new nodes, strong activations indicate similarity to an existing node/layer, and intermediate activations are deferred to deeper layers. The unresolved part is what 'integrated into \(l_c\)' concretely means. Later, for layer 1, the text says the sample is 'simply admitted into the layer for selective clustering', but it does not specify whether a new node is always appended, whether an existing representative is updated, how multiple strongly activated nodes are handled, how output values are stored or averaged when labels are similar, or what numerical rule defines 'similar' labels in this update. The appendix may define selective clustering in more detail, but the construction here invokes it as an operational step. Since the algorithm's architecture and exact-accuracy behavior depend on this choice, the main construction needs a precise update rule or a clear pointer to the exact formal rule.

comments.title: Proof of Lemma 1 relies on an unstated uniqueness/no-tie condition
comments.quote: Furthermore, for other _i_ = _j_ , we have [ _v_ 1] _i <_ 1 due to the admission conditions (1) and (2) used during _check N_ 1 _process_ . Finally, computing _y_ = _α_ 1 _[<j>]_ where _j_ = _argmaxj′_ [ _v_ 1] _j′_ , we retrieve the exact value.
comments.explanation: The intended argument is sensible: for a stored training sample, its own node activates maximally, so an argmax retrieves the corresponding stored output. Interpreting the apparent `i = j` as the intended `i \ne j`, the proof still needs a condition ensuring that no other node can also achieve activation 1. The text partly addresses this by declaring exact `[v_1]_j = 1` collisions unresolvable, but the formal training-set assumptions and tie-handling rule are not stated here. For example, duplicate identical inputs with the same label would not be ill-defined for supervised learning, yet they would create an argmax tie unless duplicates are collapsed. More generally, the proof depends on \(\sigma_{\mathrm{dsa}}(\|x-\eta\|)=1\) occurring only for the intended identical fingerprint, or on an explicit rule for ties that preserves the label. Without that assumption, the final argmax step does not by itself guarantee exact retrieval.

comments.title: Layer-k nodes appear to store the wrong activation vector, creating an off-by-one/circular definition
comments.quote: Initialize by first checking _vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ , the activation of _u[<]_[1] _[>]_ at layer _k_ for collision; if collision occurs, see next paragraph, otherwise, set _Nk_ = ( _ηk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) _, αk_ = ( _yk[<]_[1] _[>]_ ) i.e. _ηk[<]_[1] _[>] ← vk[<]_[1] _[>]_ .
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying that a deeper-layer neuron stores the fingerprint presented to that layer, which would be consistent with the earlier description that representative activations become nuclei in deeper layers. However, the later prediction rule computes layer-k activations as a function of the previous activation vector, namely [v_k]_j = σ_dsa(||v_{k-1} - η_k^{<j>}||). Under that rule, η_k^{<j>} must live in the same space as v_{k-1}, not v_k. But this construction sentence says η_k^{<1>} ← v_k^{<1>}. If v_k means the output activation of layer k, it cannot be computed before N_k exists, so the initialization is circular and dimensionally inconsistent. If the authors intend v_k^{<i>} to denote the incoming fingerprint to layer k rather than the output of layer k, that conflicts with the propagation notation used elsewhere and needs to be made explicit. As written, this affects the core reproducibility of SQANN construction.

comments.title: Collision resolution adds a current-layer vector to an earlier collided layer
comments.quote: We destroy all layers _l > lc_ and put the collided sample into _lc_ , i.e. _Nlc → concat_ ( _Nlc , vk[<m>]_ ) and _αlc → concat_ ( _αlc , yk[<m>]_ ) (push-node in the pseudo code).
comments.explanation: The intended idea is clear and useful: when a sample collides at an earlier layer, discard later layers and insert a representative for that sample at the earliest collided layer so future propagation retrieves the correct label. But the object concatenated into N_{lc} is written as v_k^{<m>}, where k is the current layer under construction. If l_c < k, this vector generally belongs to the wrong activation space. A node in layer l_c should be compared against the input/fingerprint for layer l_c, which from the propagation rule would be v_{l_c-1}^{<m>} or, depending on the authors' indexing convention, v_{l_c}^{<m>}; it should not be the activation associated with the later current layer k. The label term yk[<m>] also looks inconsistent with the earlier unused-sample labels y[<m>]. Some of this may be OCR or notation compression, but the layer mismatch would be a real algorithmic error unless clarified.

comments.title: The unused-sample sets X_i and U are not defined precisely enough for the reconstruction algorithm
comments.quote: Assume every layer _l ∈_ Λ = _{_ 1 _, . . . , k −_ 1 _}_ have been constructed using _Xi∈_ Λ _⊆ X_ . Assume there are still unused data samples i.e. _U_ = _X \ {_[] _[k] i_ =1 _[−]_[1] _[X][i][}]_[ is non-empty, obtained from samples that have been] _[ filtered to deeper layers]_
comments.explanation: I can infer the intended invariant: X_i probably denotes the subset of training samples already assigned to layer i, and U is the ordered list of samples not yet assigned because they were filtered deeper. The OCR has clearly damaged the union notation, so I am not treating the malformed symbol itself as the issue. What remains problematic is that X was earlier introduced as the input space in the finite training set {(x^{(k)},y^{(k)}) ∈ X × Y}, while here X seems to mean the finite set of training inputs or samples. The layer-specific sets X_i are also introduced only implicitly, even though collision resolution later depends on returning exactly the samples used in destroyed layers, in their original construction order. For an algorithm whose termination claim relies on order integrity, the paper needs a precise definition of these sets/lists and whether they contain inputs, input-label pairs, or indices.

comments.title: Interpolation rule is not reproducibly specified, even assuming the formula was OCR-mangled
comments.quote: Suppose _V_ 1 = [ _vm_ ] _i_ and _V_ 2 = [ _vn_ ] _j_ are the two most activated neurons, then the interpolated value can be, for example, _y_ = _V_ 1[ _αmV_ ]1 _i_ ++ _VV_ 22[ _αn_ ] _j_ .
comments.explanation: The displayed expression is visibly OCR-corrupted, and the most plausible intended formula is a weighted average such as y = (V_1[α_m]_i + V_2[α_n]_j)/(V_1+V_2). Even granting that correction, several methodological details remain underspecified. It is not stated whether the two most activated neurons are chosen globally across all layers or only from the final propagated layer; if globally, activations from different layers are being compared although their semantic roles differ. The rule also assumes that the output space supports averaging, whereas the paper earlier allows a general Y and later uses classification examples. Finally, boundary cases such as ties, zero or near-zero weights, and class-valued outputs are not defined. Since this interpolation is part of SQANN prediction on non-training inputs and is used in the experiments, the method is too vague to reproduce from the text alone.

comments.title: Completion claim uses an undefined probability statement
comments.quote: **Proposition 3** _SQANN construction completes with high probability p ≈_ 1 _. See appendix A.5.4 for (1) sketch of proof and a required assumption (2) stronger assumption needed for p_ = 1 _._
comments.explanation: The preceding discussion correctly identifies a real concern: because collisions can tear down later layers and cause reconstruction, termination is not immediate. However, the proposition's main statement is not mathematically well specified in the main text. A probability p ≈ 1 requires a probability space: for example, randomness over the sample distribution, sample ordering, perturbations, activation parameters, or tie-breaking. The phrase 'high probability p ≈ 1' is also not a standard quantified guarantee. The appendix may contain assumptions, but the main claim is important because Theorem 2 is conditioned on completed construction. At minimum, the proposition statement should state what is random and what assumption makes cycling have small or zero probability.

comments.title: Evaluation protocol leaks test data into the retraining set while claiming generalization to unseen/test samples
comments.quote: **SQANN is tested for regression on Boston Housing and Diabetes Datasets** to demonstrate its generalizability to unseen/test samples, as the following (simplified here): (1) A small subset of samples _D ⊆D_ (the first 20% of full dataset _D_ ) is used to train SQANN and 9 other regression methods. (2) Mean Squared Errors (MSE) values are measured on unused data _Dtest_ on all 10 models; we expect large errors on some test samples. (3) SQANN’s activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors _eτ_ ( _x_ ) = _|SQANN_ ( _x_ ) _− y_ 0 _| > τ_ and we treat them as out-ofdistribution (OOD) samples. These samples are considered as new distinct samples to be integrated into _D_ as the new training dataset _D[′]_ . (4) Train the 10 models, now with _D[′]_ . (5) Then MSE is measured again on _Dtest_ (yes, there will be partial overfitting).
comments.explanation: I initially read this as an active-learning or continual-learning experiment: identify difficult/OOD test points, add them to training, and check whether the model can incorporate them. That would be a reasonable way to illustrate resistance to catastrophic forgetting or sample incorporation. However, the passage frames the experiment as demonstrating “generalizability to unseen/test samples,” while step (3) explicitly selects samples from _Dtest_, step (4) adds them to the new training set _D[′]_, and step (5) evaluates again on the same _Dtest_. The parenthetical acknowledgement of “partial overfitting” helps, but it does not resolve the methodological issue: after step (4), a potentially large fraction of the evaluation set is no longer unseen. Reported post-integration MSE therefore mixes performance on genuinely held-out points with performance on points that have been incorporated into training. This substantially weakens any conclusion about generalization unless the authors separately report MSE on the remaining held-out portion not added to _D[′]_, or clearly reframe the experiment as transductive/continual incorporation rather than unseen-test generalization.

comments.title: OOD/sample-selection criterion uses ground-truth error, not just SQANN activations
comments.quote: SQANN’s activations are used to collect samples with large absolute errors _eτ_ ( _x_ ) = _|SQANN_ ( _x_ ) _− y_ 0 _| > τ_ and we treat them as out-ofdistribution (OOD) samples.
comments.explanation: The surrounding paper repeatedly emphasizes that SQANN can identify out-of-distribution samples through weak or distinctive activations, so I first tried to read this as an activation-based OOD detector. But the actual criterion shown is a supervised error threshold involving the true target value _y_0. That is not an activation-only OOD criterion and would not be available at prediction time unless the setting is explicitly active learning or post hoc dataset curation with labels. In addition, large prediction error is not equivalent to being out-of-distribution: it can arise from in-distribution noise, model bias, insufficient capacity/parameter choices, or label noise. The method may still be useful for selecting hard examples, but the passage overstates what is being demonstrated if these are called OOD samples based primarily on labelled prediction error.

comments.title: Reported Boston Housing improvements are inconsistent with Table 1 and the stated threshold
comments.quote: From Boston dataset: for _τ_ = 5, SQANN MSE improved from 9 _._ 90 to 3 _._ 08. Decision tree improves the most with _D[′]_ (7.36 to 2.07); see table 1.
comments.explanation: I checked this against Table 1 and the later explanatory note. The table appears to have rows o., e5, and e2. For SQANN, the original MSE is about 9.898, the e5 value is about 7.998, and the e2 value is about 3.076. Thus the statement “for τ = 5” improving to 3.08 seems to use the τ = 2/e2 entry, not the τ = 5/e5 entry. Similarly, the decision-tree value 2.072 is also from the e2 row, not e5. This is likely a simple threshold-label mix-up, but it directly affects the interpretation of how many test samples were integrated and how much improvement is achieved at each threshold.

comments.title: Claim that decision tree “improves the most” is not supported by the table as written
comments.quote: Decision tree improves the most with _D[′]_ (7.36 to 2.07); see table 1.
comments.explanation: The decision tree does appear to achieve the lowest reported e2 MSE in Table 1, which is a meaningful result. However, if “improves the most” means largest reduction in MSE, the table does not support that phrasing: for example, the linear model drops from 36.45 to 4.993, a much larger absolute reduction, and other models may have comparable or larger relative changes depending on the row considered. If the intended claim is that the decision tree obtains the best final MSE after augmentation, the wording should say that. As written, a careful reader could reasonably interpret “improves the most” as a numerical improvement claim, which conflicts with the table.

comments.title: SQANN/SQANN′ model-selection rule is underspecified and affects the reported comparison
comments.quote: For SQANN, the initial model trained on _D_ is kept after SQANN’ is trained on _D[′]_ . Thus, we can choose results based on the strength of activations between SQANN and SQANN’.
comments.explanation: I understand the intent: keeping the original SQANN alongside the updated SQANN may be part of the proposed resistance-to-forgetting mechanism, and activation strength could plausibly indicate which stored model is more relevant for a sample. But this sentence leaves a key experimental detail unspecified. It is unclear whether the choice between SQANN and SQANN′ is made per test sample or globally, what scalar “strength of activations” is used, how ties or interpolation cases are handled, and whether the selection rule was fixed before evaluation. Since Table 1 compares SQANN to other regressors trained on _D′_, this model-selection mechanism can materially affect the reported SQANN MSE. Without a reproducible rule, the comparison is difficult to interpret and could be unfairly favorable if the better of two SQANN outputs is chosen adaptively.

comments.title: Training subset notation reuses D for both the full dataset and the subset
comments.quote: (1) A small subset of samples _D ⊆D_ (the first 20% of full dataset _D_ ) is used to train SQANN and 9 other regression methods.
comments.explanation: The intended meaning is recoverable from context: the authors use the first 20% of the full dataset as the initial training subset, then evaluate on the unused remainder. I also considered whether the repeated _D_ might be an OCR loss of a subscript, such as _D_0 \subseteq D. Still, as printed, _D ⊆ D_ uses the same symbol for both the subset and the full dataset, and later _D′_ and _Dtest_ depend on this distinction. This is a localized notation problem, but it can confuse the experimental protocol and should be disambiguated with distinct symbols such as _D_{train}^{(0)} \subset D_{full}_.

comments.title: Unseeded randomness may not reproduce the reported numerical results exactly
comments.quote: Results are easily reproducible even with without random number seeding since data distributions are sufficiently controlled.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' likely intent: the experiments use simple, controlled synthetic or benchmark-like setups, and the qualitative behavior of TNN/SQANN should not depend sensitively on a particular random seed. The surrounding context also helps somewhat, because the authors say that code and Jupyter notebooks for the particular results are included. However, the specific claim that results are reproducible without random number seeding is stronger than what controlled distributions normally guarantee. If any reported quantities depend on randomly sampled train/test perturbations, sample ordering, or noise generation—as suggested elsewhere by the use of test-data spread and interpolation counts—then rerunning without fixed seeds can produce different datasets and different numerical values, even if the same qualitative conclusions hold. A careful reader could therefore interpret this as exact reproducibility of tables/figures, which is not ensured by distributional control alone. The fix would be to either provide fixed seeds/generated datasets for exact reproduction, or state that the qualitative trends are robust across random draws.
